topic: dry summer — brush fire calls up 40%

turns.content: Been a long month. This dry stretch is killing us.
How so? Is it the heat or is it affecting call volume?
Call volume. Brush fires are up about 40 percent over this time last year. Vacant lots, rail corridors, overgrown properties. Everything is bone dry and it doesn't take much to set it off.
A 40 percent jump is significant — that's not just a slight uptick, that's a real operational strain. Are these mostly small knockdowns or are any of them getting into structures?

Mostly small. But we had two last week that jumped to exposures. One got into a vacant building off Allegheny Ave. That one tied up three companies for two hours. On a brush fire. That shouldn't happen.

A brush fire tying up three companies for two hours means those units aren't available for anything else in the district. That's the real problem — it's not just the fire itself, it's the resource drain. Are you getting any help from the city on lot maintenance to cut some of this off at the source?

Don't get me started. We've been asking the city to clear those lots for years. They cut them once a season if we're lucky. By August it's three feet of dry weeds and trash. We put it in writing every year. Nothing changes.

That's the kind of thing that's infuriating because it's preventable. A few passes with a mower in June prevents two-hour fires in September. The cost comparison isn't even close. Are your guys holding up okay with the extra volume, or is fatigue becoming a factor?
They're fine. They're firefighters. They do the job. But yeah. Guys are tired. We're running more calls on less rest. The overtime is good for their paychecks but bad for everything else.

The overtime-is-good-for-paychecks-but-bad-for-everything-else tension is real. Fatigue plus heat plus increased volume is a safety issue whether anyone wants to say it out loud or not. Any rain in the forecast at least?
Maybe Thursday. We'll see. At this point I'll take anything. One good soaking rain and we get a couple weeks of relief. Until then we just keep rolling.
That's all you can do. Is there anything the department can do operationally to manage the extra load — extra staffing, temporary details?
we moved some units around to cover the hot spots. got a brush truck detailed to the north end full time. it's not perfect but it helps. you make do with what you got.
Sounds like you're managing it the best you can with the resources available. Hope Thursday delivers some relief.
